content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
December23, 1997
Marion Goodman
National Women’s Political Caucus of California, Inc.
3819 Palo Alto Drive
Lafayette, California 94549
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-519
Dear Ms. Goodman:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the National Women’s
Political Caucus of California regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Given the way the National Women’s Political Caucus of California is organized, are
the local chapters affiliated with the State Board?
2. The State Board is considering amending its Standing Rules and Policies that each
local must contribute 25 percent of its profits from fundraising events to the State Board. The
proposal under consideration is that the State Board would instead participate in local chapter
PAC fundraising events and share in the proceeds of the event. Would the local chapters be
affiliated with the State Board under this proposed policy?
3. How does regulation 18531.1 (d)(6) affect the formation of new local chapters in the
state?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Applying regulation 18531. 1(d)(l)(b) to your facts, there are some indications that the
local chapters are affiliated with the State Board.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-5 19
Page 2
2. If a person controls the means by which a committee raises funds, the person will be
presumed to be affiliated with the committee. Thus, if the State Board requires local chapters to
hold joint fundraisers with the State Board, the State Board will be controlling the means by
which the local chapters raise funds.
3. We do not consider “credentialing” a new local chapter to be forming a new
committee. As long as the State Board does not form the committee before it is credentialed,
regulation 1853 1.1(d)(6) will not apply. The State Board may provide information to local
jurisdictions concerning its credentialing requirements without triggering regulation
1853 1.1(d)(6). However, ifthe State Board encourages the formation of the new committee or
is, in any other way, significantly involved in the formation of the new committee, regulation
18531.l(d)(6) applies.
FACTS
Founded in 1971, the National Women’s Political Caucus is a national grassroots
volunteer organization dedicated to increasing the number ofwomen in elected and appointed
office at all levels of government. The National Women’s Political Caucus provides campaign
and leadership training and campaign assistance to women who are candidates for such public
offices as local school boards, city councils, state legislatures, offices of Governor and Attorney
General ofthe United States.
The National Women’s Political Caucus of California (“NWPC-CA”) currently has 22
chapters and 2000 members. The NWPC-CA is a nonprofit organization governed by a board of
directors (“State Board”). The State Board is made up of a representative elected by each local
chapter, and elected state officers. The directors are the only voting members. None of the
officers have a vote except the president. With the exception oftwo part-time staffmembers
who keep state membership rolls and provide clerical assistance to the State Board, volunteers do
all the work.
You provided a document entitled, “Level I’ Information Packet: Initial Briefmg for
Potential New Local Caucus Organizer” which states that, “Local caucuses are affiliated with the
state and national NWPC and support their goals and statements ofpurpose.” The State Board is
a political action committee that endorses and provides fmancial support to state candidates for
legislative and constitutional offices. The local chapters ofNWPC-CA are political action
committees that endorse and make contributions to local candidates.
When endorsing candidates, the local chapters must follow certain guidelines established
by the State Board. The endorsement guidelines require local chapters to endorse women
candidates who support the following issues: Equal Rights Amendment; choice and publicly
funded abortion; support access to childcare and dependent care programs funded from all
available sources; ending discrimination based on age, race, religion, sex, sexual orientation,
ethnic origin, marital status, socioeconomic status, and disability; comparable pay for work of


File No. A-97-519
Pae 3
comparable worth, and the elimination of sexual harassment. In addition, local chapters are
required to volunteer for statewide activities approved by the State Board.
Occasionally, local chapters contribute to statewide candidates. However, the local
chapters do not notify the State Board about their activity. They do not report to the State Board
whom they are supporting nor how much they are contributing. The local chapters are free to
decide how, when or whether to make contributions to state races. Generally, when local
chapters contribute to statewide candidates, they contribute to candidates endorsed by the State
Board. However, they are free to contribute to statewide candidates that are not endorsed by the
State Board.
The State Board derives most of its funds through contributions from local caucuses.
Annually, each local caucus must send to the State Board 25 percent of the profit from every
general committee event or fundraising activity. The State Board is considering changing its
policy requiring local chapters to contribute to the State Board. The proposal under
consideration involves the State Board participating in fundraising events with local chapters and
sharing in the proceeds ofthe events.
To become a local chapter, a committee must meet certain guidelines established by the
State Board. Once the local committee meets such guidelines, the committee is “credentialed”
by the State Board. To be credentialed, a committee must do the following: have at least 20 paid
members; provide membership information to the State Board; submit proof ofpublicity ofthe
first three formation meetings; provide evidence of an Affirmative Action Program; and provide
and postmark all required documentation at least ten days prior to each board meeting.
ANALYSIS
I. General Rule
Proposition 208 establishes various contribution limitations and restrictions. (Sections
85302-85313.) Pursuant to section 85311, all contributions made by affiliated entities are
aggregated for purposes of the contribution limits. (Section 85311, Regulation 18531.1(b).)
You would like to know whether the NV’[PC-CA’s State Board is affiliated with local
chapters ofNWPC-CA. You provided a document entitled, “Level I’ Information Packet: Initial
Briefing for Potential New Local Caucus Organizer which states that, “Local caucuses are
affiliated with the state and national NWPC and support their goals and statements ofpurpose.”
We think this statement is some evidence that the local caucuses are affiliated entities of the State
Board. Regulation 18531.1 provides a presumption of affiliation if at least one of the following
criteria apply:
“(1) If during the twelve-month period prior to making a
campaign contribution to a candidate, a person possesses either of
the following with respect to an entity:


File No. A-97-519
Page 4
(A) Ownership of an interest of more than 50 percent in
voting rights, shares or securities ofthe entity or of a person having
control over such entity; or
(B) The authority, power, or ability to direct or control the
activities ofthe entity or a person having control over such entity.
(2) Each of the entities has the same, or a majority of the same,
persons as board membçrs or principal officers and this indicates a
formal relationship.
(3) The entities in question are political committees that have
received contributions and receive contributions from the same
person or persons, which contributions represent a majority ofthe
contributions received by each of the political committees in a
calendar year.
(4) A person directly or indirectly provides a majority of an
entity’s funds, services, or goods on a regular or ongoing basis, for
less than full consideration.
(5) One ofthe entities is a sponsored committee ofthe other
entity (see Government Code section 82048.7).
(6) A person has, after December 31, 1996, formed an entity for
the purpose ofmaking contributions to California state or local
candidates.” (Regulation 18531.1(d).)
II. Does the State Board Control the Activities of the Local Chapters?
Pursuant to regulation 18531.1 (d)(1)(B), a person is presumed to be affiliated with an
entity ifthe person has the authority, power, or ability to direct and control the activities of the
entity for a period of one year before the malcing of a campaign contribution. The most
significant activity conducted by a political action committee is the expenditure of committee
funds. Therefore, if a person directs and controls an entity’s expenditures, the person will be
presumed to be affiliated with the entity. A second significant activity conducted by a committee
is raising committee funds. Thus, if a person directs and controls the means by which a
committee raises funds, the person will be presumed to be affiliated with the entity.
A. Volunteering for Statewide Activities
Local chapters are expected to volunteer for statewide activities that are approved by the
State Board. You have not indicated the type of activities for which local chapters must
volunteer. This obligation may be another indication of affiliation if the activities involve the
expenditure of local chapter funds or raising local chapter funds.
B. Endorsement Guidelines
Generally, committees that endorse candidates only contribute to candidates they endorse
rather than to candidates they do not endorse. Therefore, under certain circumstances, if a person
controls the procedures by which an entity makes endorsements, the person may be affiliated


File No. A-97-519
PageS
with the entity. Local chapters of the NWPC-CA endorse and make contributions to local
candidates. When endorsing local candidates, the local chapters must follow certain guidelines
established by the State Board. The endorsement guidelines require local chapters to endorse
women candidates who support the following issues: Equal Rights Amendment; choice and
publicly funded abortion; support access to childcare and dependent care programs funded from
all available sources; ending discrimination based on age, race, religion, sex, sexual orientation,
ethnic origin, marital status, socioeconomic status, and disability; comparable pay for work of
comparable worth, and the elimination of sexual harassment. These general guidelines do not
indicate affiliation.
C. Contributions to Statewide Candidates
Occasionally, local chapters contribute to statewide candidates. However, the local
chapters do not notify the State Board about their activity. They do not report to the State Board
whom they are supporting nor how much they are contributing. The local chapters are free to
decide how, when or whether to make contributions to state races. Generally, when local
chapters contribute to statewide candidates, they contribute to candidates endorsed by the State
Board. However, they are free to contribute to statewide candidates that are not endorsed by the
State Board. Under these facts, there is no indication of affiliation when local chapters make
contributions to statewide candidates.
D. Fundraising Activities
The State Board derives most of its funds through contributions from local caucuses.
Annually, each local caucus must send to the State Board 25 percent of the profit from every
general committee event or fundraising activity. Because this policy involves the expenditure of
local chapter funds, it is an indication of affiliation. However, the State Board is considering
changing its policy requiring local chapters to contribute to the State Board. The proposal under
consideration involves the State Board participating in fundraising events with local chapters and
sharing in the proceeds of the events. If the State Board requires local chapters to hold joint
fundraisers with the State Board, the State Board will be controlling the means by which the
local chapters raise funds. Thus, ifthe State Board requires the local chapters to hold joint
fundraisers with the State Board, there would be an indication of affiliation.
III. Political Committees that Receive Contributions from the Same Persons
Pursuant to regulation 18531.1 (d)(3) entities that are political committees are presumed
to be affiliated if each of the committees receive a majority of its contributions from the same
contributors. The State Board derives most of its funds from the 22 local chapters and its
members. Regulation 18531.1 (d)(3) does not apply where a committee receives funds on a
proportionate basis from 22 different sources that are not affiliated.


File No. A-97-519
Page 6
IV. New Committees Formed after January 1, 1997
Pursuant to regulation 1853 1.1(d)(6), a person is presumed to be affiliated with a
committee ifthe person “forms” the committee for the purpose ofmaking contributions to
statewide or local candidates in California. You would like to know how this provision affects
the NWPC-CA forming new local chapters. Ifthe State Boardforms a new local chapter after
January 1, 1997, the new chapter will be deemed affiliated with the State Board.
To become a local chapter, a committee must meet certain guidelines established by the
State Board. Once the local committee meets such guidelines, the committee is “credentialed”
by the State Board. To be credentialed, a committee must do the following: have at least 20 paid
members; provide membership information to the State Board; submit proofofpublicity of the
first three formation meetings; provide evidence of an Affirmative Action Program; and provide
and postmark all required documentation at least ten days prior to each board meeting.
We do not consider “credentialing” a new local chapter to be forming a new committee.
As long as the State Board does not form the committee before it is credentialed, regulation
1853 1.1(d)(6) will not apply. The State Board may provide information to local jurisdictions
concerning its credentialing requirements without triggering regulation 1853 1.1(d)(6). However,
ifthe State Board encourages the formation of the new committee or is, in any other way,
significantly involved in the formation ofthe new committee, regulation 1853 1.1(d)(6) applies.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
